title: The Eve Of Revolution by Algernon Charles Swinburne — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
The trumpets of the four winds of the world
From the ends of the earth blow battle; the night heaves,
With breasts palpitating and wings refurled,
With passion of couched limbs, as one who grieves
Sleeping, and in her sleep she sees uncurled
Dreams serpent-shapen, such as sickness weaves,
Down the wild wind of vision caught and whirled,
Dead leaves of sleep, thicker than autumn leaves,
meaning: "The Eve Of Revolution" by Algernon Charles Swinburne is a sad and love and deep and nature and inspirational poem consisting of 510 lines. The poet explores themes of sad and love and deep and nature and inspirational through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "The trumpets of the four winds of the world From the ends of the earth blow battle; the night heaves...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Algernon Charles Swinburne's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
